claim: Physical activity levels have been associated with the differences in VO2max between black and white youth.

result.claim: Physical activity levels have been associated with the differences in VO2max between black and white youth.
result.reasoning: The claim is strongly supported by evidence from paper ID 18537148, "Comparison of Maximal Oxygen Consumption Between Black and White Prepubertal and Pubertal Children". The abstract of this paper explicitly states that "Black prepubertal and pubertal children had lower VO2max values when compared with white children... This difference in VO2max could be attributed at least in part to comparatively lower [Hb] and more sedentary lifestyle in the black children." This directly links physical activity levels (sedentary lifestyle) to the observed differences in VO2max between black and white youth. The study involved 45 prepubertal and 42 pubertal, clinically normal black and white children, and assessed physical activity/inactivity by questionnaire.

evidence.text: Perceived age correlates with functional and molecular ageing phenotypes.

evidence.text: Low physical activity increased with SDI.
evidence.source: Global, regional, and national comparative risk assessment of 79 behavioural, environmental and occupational, and metabolic risks or clusters of risks, 1990–2015: a systematic analysis for the Global Burden of Disease Study 2015

evidence.text: Graduates self-identified as underrepresented racial/ethnic minorities in the other nongeneralist specialties category (vs white) were less likely to be board certified, with an AOR of 0.79 (95% CI, 0.64-0.96).

evidence.text: Non-Hispanic blacks had an obesity prevalence of 36.8%.

evidence.text: Non-Hispanic blacks had 1.19-3.27 and 1.15-2.68 times higher rates than non-Hispanic whites for 11 of 15 indicators in white- and Hispanic-serving hospitals.

evidence.text: The LL children had lower intakes of vitamin A and calcium.
